title: Choosing for the Task
concept: The best backbone depends on the task and product: classification, detection, segmentation, and mobile apps need different balances of feature strength, spatial detail, multi-scale reasoning, and deployment efficiency — which is why pretrained backbones and transfer learning become so useful next.

exercises.prompt: Recap: which situation most strongly favors an edge-friendly backbone?

options.text: A phone app that must work privately and offline on the device
A centralized server with roomy hardware budgets
A task with no images at all

exercises.explain: Exactly — now add one more filter on top of hardware choice: what task the model must solve.

cards.title: Seeing “what” is not the same as seeing “where”
cards.body: A classifier can look at the whole image and answer “What is this?” A detector must also answer “Where is it?” A segmenter goes even further and marks pixels. Those jobs reuse the backbone idea, but they put different pressure on what kinds of features are most useful.

cards.title: Task type changes what you need from the backbone
cards.body: Classification often cares most about strong whole-image features. Detection and segmentation care more about location-aware features and detail across scales, because the model must reason about position as well as category. Mobile apps add another requirement: the backbone must still fit the device budget.

cards.title: Different exams need different skills
cards.body: Answering a multiple-choice question, drawing a map, and coloring every region are all “school tasks,” but they stress different abilities. Vision tasks are similar. Classification, detection, and segmentation all use visual understanding, yet they ask for different forms of output and different levels of spatial detail.

cards.title: Task plus environment narrows the choice fast
cards.body: A simple plant classifier for a phone can often start with a lightweight backbone. A street-scene detector or medical segmenter may justify a stronger backbone if small objects or fine boundaries matter. The same family can appear in several settings, but the final choice always depends on the task-hardware pair.

cards.title: There is no single “best backbone for vision”
cards.body: Asking for the best backbone without naming the task is like asking for the best vehicle without naming the road. A strong answer includes the job, the latency budget, the deployment target, and how much labeled data you have.

cards.title: Whole-image tasks vs. location-heavy tasks
cards.body: Different tasks emphasize different kinds of visual evidence.

left.text:
Whole-image label is enough
Often okay with simpler output head
Light backbones can work well on mobile

right.text:
Need location-aware features
Care more about scale and detail
Often justify stronger feature extraction

exercises.prompt: Which task most clearly needs the model to understand both what an object is and where it is?
exercises.explain: Correct — detection needs category plus location.

exercises.prompt: True or false: the same backbone idea can support classification, detection, and segmentation when paired with different heads and training setups.
exercises.explain: True — that reuse is a big reason backbones are central in modern vision pipelines.

exercises.sentence: Instead of training a backbone from scratch, engineers often start from a ___ model and fine-tune it.
exercises.explain: Exactly — reusing a pretrained backbone is one of the most common practical tricks in vision.

exercises.prompt: Which project plan sounds most practical?

options.text: For a phone-based plant classifier with limited data, start with a lightweight pretrained backbone and fine-tune it
Train the largest possible model from scratch even though data and hardware are both limited
Ignore the task type and choose a backbone only by whichever name sounds coolest

exercises.explain: Yes — task, hardware, and data availability should all influence the backbone choice.

exercises.prompt: You need a phone app that identifies plant diseases from leaf photos. The dataset is not huge, the app should feel fast, and the model will run on-device. What is the strongest plan?

options.text: Start with a lightweight pretrained backbone, attach a classification head, and fine-tune it for the plant task
Train the heaviest possible detector from scratch, because mobile apps never care about speed or data limits
Skip the backbone and hope the classification head learns leaf features alone
Choose a cloud-only model even though the app must work on-device

exercises.explain: Exactly. Good backbone choice depends on the task, the hardware, and the amount of data. That answer naturally leads to the next idea: transfer learning, where you reuse a pretrained backbone instead of training all visual features from scratch.
